book_claims.claim: Multiple studies have demonstrated vitamin C to possess cancer-fighting properties, although its effectiveness in preventing cancer is controversial; high-dose protocol (6 g daily for a few weeks every 6 months) discussed with oncologist for cancer prevention
book_claims.mechanism: Vitamin C may enhance immune function, support collagen synthesis, and act as an antioxidant against DNA-damaging reactive oxygen species

book_claims.claim: Alzheimer's disease patients have lower levels of vitamin C; deficiency may contribute to Alzheimer's disease
book_claims.mechanism: Vitamin C is a potent water-soluble antioxidant; deficiency may accelerate oxidative neuronal damage contributing to brain aging
book_claims.study_ref: Lopes da Silva et al., Alzheimer's and Dementia, 2014

supplementary_research.finding: Meta-analysis (PMID 24377461) found plasma vitamin C levels are inversely associated with all-cause mortality; individuals in the highest quartile had 20% lower mortality risk

High-dose intravenous vitamin C in cancer patients (PMID 26609001) showed improved quality of life and reduced inflammation markers; consistent with Longo's discussion of its potential cancer-fighting properties

Vitamin C is the primary water-soluble antioxidant; regenerates vitamin E, enhances non-heme iron absorption, is essential for collagen and carnitine synthesis; deficiency linked to impaired immunity and accelerated aging

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Water-soluble; absorption is dose-dependent, near 100% at low doses, ~50% at 1250 mg; excess is renally excreted
Also absorbed via glucose transporters; rapidly reduced back to ascorbate intracellularly
Co-present in citrus and berries; may enhance ascorbate bioavailability and provide synergistic antioxidant effects

synergies.description: Vitamin C reduces ferric to ferrous iron, enhancing non-heme iron absorption by up to 3-fold; critical pairing for plant-based diets
Vitamin C regenerates oxidized vitamin E (tocopheroxyl radical) back to its active form, creating a synergistic antioxidant network
Vitamin C is an essential cofactor for prolyl and lysyl hydroxylase enzymes required for collagen cross-linking and structural integrity
